title: Self-Learn Backend Development: DDD and Hexagonal Architecture in Node.js, Go, and Bun
description: Build one small backend in Node.js + TypeScript, Go, or Bun + TypeScript. The example course keeps the language path flexible while making the architectural decisions explicit.

sections.title: Start a path
sections.goal: Choose a language, confirm a workspace, and make a minimal status route visible.
sections.action: Create the agreed folder structure and one health/status route in the selected workspace. Run the suggested development command yourself.

questions.prompt: Why should the course runner and the application workspace be different folders? Name one risk the boundary avoids.
State your selected language, workspace, development command, and one reason progress is keyed to that path.

sections.title: Model the domain
sections.goal: Protect one business invariant before choosing routes or tables.
sections.action: Write a short ubiquitous-language note and implement one domain type or aggregate with no framework or database import.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a business invariant and HTTP input validation? Give one example of each.
Name one invariant your domain owns, one transport validation rule, and why they are different responsibilities.

sections.title: Draw the hexagon
sections.goal: Make dependency direction visible through domain, application, ports, and adapters.
sections.action: Create one use case, one port owned by the inner layer, and one in-memory adapter. Wire them together at the outer composition point.

questions.prompt: What dependency direction is reversed when a domain object imports a database client, and why is that costly?
For one dependency, name the caller, port owner, and adapter. Why is the port shaped by the inside need?

sections.title: Make an API useful
sections.goal: Translate one HTTP request into a use case without mixing transport, business rules, and persistence.
sections.action: Create one command endpoint plus a status route. Map malformed transport input and known domain failures deliberately.

questions.prompt: Why is a handler that holds business rules, SQL, and JSON formatting difficult to change or test?
Trace one request from HTTP input to a domain decision and back. Where do malformed input and invariant violations stop?

sections.title: Persist through a port
sections.goal: Swap an in-memory repository for persistence without leaking storage into domain code.
sections.action: Implement one persistence adapter, map data at its edge, and document a transaction boundary for one use case.

questions.prompt: How can a domain identity differ from a database primary key, even when both happen to use the same value?
Which layer knows the database library, which layer defines the persistence need, and how does that protect an invariant?

sections.title: Prove behaviour
sections.goal: Use fast tests to protect domain rules and a small number of boundaries.
sections.action: Add one domain test, one use-case test with an in-memory adapter, and one HTTP boundary test. Run the suite yourself.

questions.prompt: What can a fast domain test prove that a route-level test might obscure?
For your domain, use-case, and HTTP test, state one behaviour each proves and one thing it does not prove.

sections.title: Handle failure deliberately
sections.goal: Differentiate rejection, transient failure, duplicate delivery, and unsafe retries.
sections.action: Model one expected rejection, choose an idempotency boundary for one side effect, and test a retry or duplicate decision.

questions.prompt: Why is catch-everything-and-retry unsafe for a backend with external side effects?
Classify one failure as domain rejection, transient failure, or duplicate. Is a retry safe, and what makes it safe?

sections.title: Observe and ship
sections.goal: Make the small backend diagnosable and explainable to another developer.
sections.action: Add minimal structured logging, keep a health route separate from correctness, and write a short runbook in the workspace.

questions.prompt: Why can logging every request body be both unhelpful and unsafe?
Name one diagnostic signal, one value you intentionally do not log, and one condition a health route cannot prove.
